claim: A live operator, executing at runtime through shell commands or tool calls, shall confine all file mutations to its launch working directory -- never touching the host harness or global configuration unless the user explicitly requests otherwise; this live-conduct discipline is not statically checkable (no committed-code test can observe a runtime shell-out) and is enforced as honest PROSE.
why: SPLIT 2026-07-13 (task #115, decision brief docs/reviews/2026-07-13-category-c2-decision-brief.md section 6 option (a')): this requirement was split into two atoms because its single claim bundled two genuinely different things -- (1) committed framework CODE writing into the host home (statically, mechanically checkable) and (2) a live agent shelling out at RUNTIME to touch the home dir (not statically checkable). The structural/code half is now its own ENFORCED atom R-committed-code-no-home-writes (refines this one), enforced by a Go AST scanner (TestLaunchDirWriteScope_NoHomeWriteColocation in internal/selfcheck/launch_dir_scope_test.go) re-ported from the original Python test_launch_dir_write_scope.py. THIS atom keeps the residual live-agent-runtime-conduct half (2): an operator shelling out through bash/tool-calls at runtime and touching the home directory is not statically observable -- no committed-code test can see it -- so it is honest discipline-of-prose, the same category as R-agent-conduct-is-rules-not-tests, which it now mirrors (enforceability ENFORCEABLE -> INHERENTLY_PROSE, since the live-conduct half is the highest abstraction that code cannot verify). Steward verdict 2026-07-05 stands verbatim: 'cah вообще нельзя трогать - он ведь обновится всё сотрет. И он в праве быть собой, без наших вмешательств' + 'нужно создать правило, что работаем строго в папке запуска, пока пользователь не попросит иного'. Trigger: the operator proposed setup_context_hook.py --patch-global --apply, which surgically patched the global host configuration -- a double fault: (1) it violates host sovereignty (the harness is a guest environment we inhabit, not own), and (2) it is futile, since a host update overwrites the patch anyway. The framework is a guest in the host's home: it lives strictly within the repository it was launched in; everything outside is sovereign and untouched absent an explicit user request. History: this ID was briefly ENFORCED 2026-07-10 via the Python AST scanner, reverted to PROSE 2026-07-12 when that scanner was not carried forward; the 2026-07-13 split moves the mechanically-checkable half to R-committed-code-no-home-writes (ENFORCED) and keeps the genuinely-unobservable live-conduct half here as INHERENTLY_PROSE -- more honest than a blanket INHERENTLY_PROSE on the original bundled claim (which would have been a regression from its ENFORCED state 3 days earlier), per decision brief section 6.
